elements.sent-2411-001: We prove that, when $n$ goes to infinity, Kostant's problem has negative answer for almost all simple highest weight modules in the principal block of the BGG category $\mathcal{O}$ for the Lie algebra $\mathfrak{sl _n(\mathbb{C )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-002: For $n>1$, the elements of the symmetric group $S_n$ naturally index the simple highest weight modules in the principal block $\mathcal{O _0$ of the Bernstein-Gelfand-Gelfand category $\mathcal{O}$ for the Lie algebra $\mathfrak{sl _n(\mathbb{C )$, see \cite{BGG,Hu .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-003: For $w\in S_n$, we denote by $L_w$ the corresponding simple highest weight modules.
elements.sent-2411-004: For each such $L_w$, there is a classical question, usually referred to as {\em Kostant's problem , which asks whether the universal enveloping algebra of $\mathfrak{sl _n(\mathbb{nC )$ surjects onto the algebra of adjointly-finite linear endomorphism of $L_w$, see \cite{Jo .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-005: We will call $L_w$ (and also $w$) {\em Kostant positive if the answer is ``yes'' and {\em Kostant negative if the answer is ``no''.}}
elements.sent-2411-006: In general, the answer to Kostant's problem for $L_w$ is not known, however, many special cases are settled, see \cite{Ma23 for an overview.}
elements.sent-2411-007: It is known, see \cite{MS2 ,n that the answer to Kostant's problem for $L_w$ is an invariant of the Kazhdan-Lusztig left cell of $w$.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Each such left cell contains a unique involution.
elements.sent-2411-009: Therefore, in principle, it is enough to solve Kostant's problem for involutions in $S_n$.
elements.sent-2411-011: The following two conjectures were proposed in \cite[Subsection~6.9]{MMM :.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Almost all elements in $S_n$ are Kostant negative in the sense that we have $\frac{\mathbf{p _n {n!}\to 0$ when $n\to \infty$.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Almost all involutions in $nS_n$ are Kostant negative in the sense that we have $\frac{\mathbf{pi _n {\mathbf{i _n}\to 0$ when $n\to \infty$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: The main evidence for this conjecture was a complete answer to Kostant problem for fully commutative elements obtained in \cite{MMM using combinatorics of the Temperley-Lieb algebra.}
elements.sent-2411-015: This answer was detailed enough to prove the analogues of the above conjectures for fully commutative elements.
elements.sent-2411-016: In the present note we confirm both conjectures and prove the following:.
elements.sent-2411-019: Recall that a permutation $w\in S_n$ is called {\em consecutively $2143$-avoiding provided that the disjunction, over all $i=1,2,\dots,n-3$, of the inequalities n \begin{displaymath w(i+1)<w(i)<w(i+4)<w(i+3) \end{displaymath is false.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Our principal observation behind Theorems~\ref{thm3 and \ref{thm4 is the following necessary condition for Kostant positivity:.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: If $L_w$ is Kostant positive, then $w$ is consecutively $2143$-avoiding.
elements.sent-2411-022: This observation crystallized during our work on two projects related to Kostant's problem for longest elements in parabolic subgroups and for $S_7$ which are to appear in \cite{CM1,CM2 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: With Proposition~\ref{prop5 at hand, Theorem~\ref{thm3 is fairly straightforward while Theorem~\ref{thm4 requires some analytical estimates of the number of consecutively $2143$-avoiding involutions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: It is well-known that $2143$-avoiding involutions are enumerated by Motzkin numbers, see \cite{GPP , however, we did not manage to find much information about consecutively $2143$-avoiding involutions.}
